## [00:00 - 03:05]

Hey everyone, it's Lane with the Mastermind team and I am back with a quick tutorial on Zoom. Zoom is a free online teleconferencing software. You're probably familiar with it already. I'm sure you've attended at least one Zoom meeting. But if you haven't, or even if you have, we're going to go through some basics for Zoom today. And with that level, you get unlimited one-on-one meetings. Your meetings are limited to 40 minutes at a time, but you can have unlimited numbers of those. I think it's 100 people you can have in your Zoom meeting at one time. You also have some fun options like virtual backgrounds and breakouts for your attendees and things like that. I have found that for the most part, the Zoom basic level is totally adequate for what most people need. If you find you are regularly going over 40 minutes, you might want to think about upgrading, but especially as you're learning Zoom, just start with the free option, the free basic level at zoom.us. Now I have Zoom installed to my computer desktop, so all I do to open up the program is to double click on the icon, which is in my menu bar, and then I come to this home screen. And you can see that there's a couple options here. We can start a new meeting. We can join a meeting that someone else has created or that's already in progress. We can schedule a meeting for the future and we can share our screen. Before we go into starting a new meeting, I want to show you some of the basic settings that you're going to want to be familiar with. Here are a couple things that I like to play with though. The background and effects. And when you open that up, that shows you how you can put a green screen behind you. You can also blur your background. You can be like Bugs Life and be in the middle of the grass. Or you could even create a custom background that has your logo and your business name on it. That would be kind of cool. So we're going to go with none for now and I'm going to go off of the background and effects. I just want to show you that is one possibility. The next thing I want to show you is the recording options. Now you can record to your desktop, which means a local recording, or you can save it to the cloud. I like to keep it on my desktop because it's just much easier for me to access it and find it. So what happens is every time I start a meeting, I have it set to automatically record my meetings. Nobody is where you're going to go if you want to control who comes into your meetings and what privileges those people have when they come into your meetings. So you can lock your meeting meeting, nobody can come in even if they have the link to it. You can enable waiting room, which means if somebody tries to come into your room, they go into kind of a purgatory. You get notified that they're waiting and think of it just as a virtual waiting room, they're in the lobby and when you click allow, they will come into the actual video room. You can hide profile pics if you want to make everybody more anonymous and you can choose what privileges your participants have. ## [06:07 - 09:10]

You can ask everybody to unmute. You can lock the meeting. You can control some things from here as well. So play around with that and see what your capabilities are. Then you can close down this side panel by just clicking on this X up here. The next button over is the chat. This is really fun because if you're holding a meeting and you don't want everybody talking at once verbally because that could be really chaotic if everybody's talking over each other, you can ask people to comment in the chat. You can ask them to submit their questions in the chat. That allows you a little more control over the process. So for instance, if I'm holding a mastermind and I'm doing a hot seat with someone, people might want to make their own suggestions or comments, but I don't want them speaking up and verbally inputting or interrupting the hot seat. They can put their comments and questions over here in the chat. And again, to close that down, I just click the X and it closes that down. The next button over is the share screen. This one is really important because if I am using Zoom to record my course as I teach in the course module for mastermind business system, this is how I would do it. I would click on share screen. And I can scroll through it while I'm sharing this, this whatever's on my desktop with everybody who's in my Zoom meeting. Up here at the top, you can see all my control options. Something else that I can do is if I go to the advanced options, I can share just a portion of my desktop screen. Now, why would I want to do that? I could share and select just a portion of my screen to share and have my notes over on one side. So I'm sharing the presentation and nobody else can see my notes but me. ## [12:11 - 12:57]

And always, always, always test things things out before you go live with them. So let's say you've got your first mastermind coming up with 12 people. You want to use breakout rooms. Don't make that actual meeting the first time you ever try using breakout rooms. Do a few sample sessions first, just on your own, trial runs, so you can work out of the kinks. So by the time you go live with your paying clients, you are ready to go. So as you can see, there's a ton of things you can do with Zoom and we've touched just on the very basics of it here. But again, go in, create your own account, Zoom.us, play around with it, see where things are recorded, see how you can download it, find what you need, and get ready to host your profitable course or mastermind or coaching session.
